Present novel invented entities with facts stated only in context, then ask a question whose exact answer the generating program knows. Implemented: slm.worldgen generates the tasks and evaluate.binding_probe scores them by exact match, with no judge.

A task states consistent facts about invented people, places, and objects in a context paragraph (residence, workplace, ownership, storage, pairwise age and size comparisons, with some surface forms inverting the stated direction), then asks for one back: who owns a thing, where it is kept, which of two is older or larger. Nothing is answerable from world knowledge, so exact-match accuracy isolates whether the model binds and retrieves context-local information.

Implemented as the first working output of the world-state generator (node 36): worldgen samples a small consistent world, verbalizes a fragment through varied templates, and emits the question with its ground-truth answer and, for comparisons, the distractor; evaluate.binding_probe scores the gold name appearing in the head of the completion before the distractor. This is cheaper and more reliable than judge scoring, and more informative than the accuracy probe (node 28), which tests parametric recall a tiny model cannot have; binding tests the processor capability (node 32) the project actually targets. It also serves as the coherence gauge for the gate in node 45: when binding exact-match rises meaningfully above zero, the model has crossed from surface fluency to usable context-binding. Extensions not yet built: multi-hop questions (combining two stated facts), longer contexts, and using the same generator to emit training documents at scale.
